messages.author: ClinchSparkleBiscuit
messages.text: Just thinking ahead, but it seems a big vulnerability to a meshcore network is malicious flooding. Is it possible to add a feature to repeaters to have a list of blocked public keys (companions or repeaters)? If this is already a feature I apologize, I looked in the docs but didn’t find it.
messages.content_html: Just thinking ahead, but it seems a big vulnerability to a meshcore network is malicious flooding. Is it possible to add a feature to repeaters to have a list of blocked public keys (companions or repeaters)? If this is already a feature I apologize, I looked in the docs but didn’t find it.
messages.reactions_html: 5

messages.author: Ceres Station 🌑
messages.text: thoughts: who gets to decide who can and can't use the mesh? are you in charge? am i? is obama in charge of the mesh? public messages don't have keys attached pathed messages use the prefix. It's very easy to quickly and repeatedly change the prefix, even from a distance, if one knows what one is doing if one wants to bring down the mesh, all one has to do is interfere with the radio. blasting at 910 mhz or whatever local flavor will be enough
messages.content_html:
thoughts:
  • who gets to decide who can and can't use the mesh? are you in charge? am i? is obama in charge of the mesh?
  • public messages don't have keys attached
  • pathed messages use the prefix. It's very easy to quickly and repeatedly change the prefix, even from a distance, if one knows what one is doing
  • if one wants to bring down the mesh, all one has to do is interfere with the radio. blasting at 910 mhz or whatever local flavor will be enough

messages.author: ClinchSparkleBiscuit
messages.text: I think the repeater owners have authority over how it is used. If the owner wants to block a user then that is the owner prerogative. I understand the spirit of openness with meshcore, but at some point there will be turds that must be dealt with. You may live in a gum drop world where you trust everyone to do the right thing and play nice, but all it takes is one disgruntled user to hop on the mesh and flood it.

messages.author: ViezeVingertjes
messages.text: With the 2 byte ID's you could block an specific repeater etc, theoretically you could even now. But if one's intent is to harm, then one could just generate thousands of keys and adverts randomly and DDoS the whole network. It would be more usefull for when a repeater goes into a bootloop, to silence it for a period untill its fixed i think. (edited)
messages.content_html: With the 2 byte ID's you could block an specific repeater etc, theoretically you could even now. It would be more usefull for when a repeater goes into a bootloop, to silence it for a period untill its fixed i think. (edited)

Do we wanna be waiting for a firmware release to come out addressing the issue or do we want to be proactive and ready to deal with the threat.

messages.text: You may have better luck blocking the prefix chains/sequences from your end. You identify the stable part of the abuse chain and block upstream of that, the varying part. Of course the length of that can be anything 1 bytes/I'D up to 64

messages.author: Matiko
messages.text: It might actually be a solid idea. The owner shouldn't necessarily be the one deciding who can or can't join the mesh; instead, it should be more of an open infrastructure. However, an anti-spam system would likely be essential to prevent network congestion. While storing reputation data or blacklists might require significant memory as the network scales, at some point, it becomes a necessity for stability. Given the ESP32-S3's resources, we might find a way to implement this efficiently. In this case owner can decide how often their repeater accept messages from clients. Propably it should be some fixed range of allowed value.
messages.content_html: It might actually be a solid idea. The owner shouldn't necessarily be the one deciding who can or can't join the mesh; instead, it should be more of an open infrastructure. However, an anti-spam system would likely be essential to prevent network congestion. While storing reputation data or blacklists might require significant memory as the network scales, at some point, it becomes a necessity for stability. Given the ESP32-S3's resources, we might find a way to implement this efficiently. In this case owner can decide how often their repeater accept messages from clients. Propably it should be some fixed range of allowed value.

messages.author: sam_uk
messages.text: seems like there might be some useful failsafes with (byte 66)? One way to stop an attacker from flooding a massive area is to implement TTL Capping in your firmware. Even if an attacker sends a packet with a TTL of 255, your repeater can "tame" the packet by overriding that byte.
messages.content_html: seems like there might be some useful failsafes with (byte 66)? One way to stop an attacker from flooding a massive area is to implement TTL Capping in your firmware. Even if an attacker sends a packet with a TTL of 255, your repeater can "tame" the packet by overriding that byte.

messages.author: sam_uk
messages.text: I think 'blocking' is the wrong framing TBH. We're just asking repeaters to run more checks before forwarding a given packet, that's not 'blocking' in my view, but rule based dropping of problematic looking packets
messages.content_html: I think 'blocking' is the wrong framing TBH. We're just asking repeaters to run more checks before forwarding a given packet, that's not 'blocking' in my view, but rule based dropping of problematic looking packets

messages.author: sam_uk
messages.text: My robot says: Extract the Key: Look at Bytes 2–33 of every incoming packet. Count the Frequency: Maintain a tiny table in RAM: [Public Key] -> [Packets per Minute]. Apply the Ceiling: If Packets per Minute > 15, drop everything from that Key for the next 60 seconds.
messages.content_html: My robot says: Extract the Key: Look at Bytes 2–33 of every incoming packet. Count the Frequency: Maintain a tiny table in RAM: [Public Key] -> [Packets per Minute]. Apply the Ceiling: If Packets per Minute > 15, drop everything from that Key for the next 60 seconds.

messages.author: Jim diGriz
messages.text: To deal with bad actor traffic, I suspect your only (handsoff) option is QoS with something like a multi-queue FIFO ( https://tldp.org/HOWTO/Traffic-Control-HOWTO/classless-qdiscs.html#qs-pfifo_fast)
messages.content_html: To deal with bad actor traffic, I suspect your only (handsoff) option is QoS with something like a multi-queue FIFO (https://tldp.org/HOWTO/Traffic-Control-HOWTO/classless-qdiscs.html#qs-pfifo_fast)

messages.author: Ed
messages.text: Dumb MC user here who doesn't know how to code but read thru this ... makes me look forward to the time when the newness of MC wears off and the junk traffic drops and I also am looking forward to exchanging keys with more users so more of the traffic sent over time is DM with the routing applied rather than flood. In other words, I am hoping the MC network will survive it's rapid growth in traffic so it can get to the point when the actual flood traffic subsides. Fingers crossed! (edited)

messages.author: ClinchSparkleBiscuit
messages.text: Hello Novie user here. Yes, scalability is a key feature MC that caters toward lower telemetry traffic and timed companion (MT Client) adverts. our growing mesh in eastern Nebraska exploded in a few months. We currently run a spread factor of 9 and it takes a loooong time for packet transmission. i.e. getting clear air time is becoming a scarce commodity as the mesh grows. I put in this request as a proactive measure because some bad apple spammers can cause alot of headaches. I also like the scope feature of MC as it allows flood messages intended only for a specific area to stay local and not flood to our capitol city Lincoln. I am really digging MC and is looking great for growing build-outs. (edited)

messages.author: Beanow
messages.text: I think rate limiting. https://forum.letsmesh.net/t/236 Started on it a bit, but some issues to figure out
messages.content_html: I think rate limiting. https://forum.letsmesh.net/t/236 Started on it a bit, but some issues to figure out
embeds_html:
Rate limiting algorithms
Currently I’m experimenting with the idea of adding rate limits to repeaters. As motivation, not only do we see scaling issues, I think the mesh is very easy to degrade with intentional DoS attacks. Generate 1000 fake repeater adverts and the whole network will happily amplify it for you. Token bucket by sender hash & priority The idea I was...
messages.reactions_html: 1
